sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=50, col=30), MOUSE(row=55, col=25), MOUSE(row=55, col=35), MOUSE(row=60, col=30).

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: 8 of 9 cells are green. Two cells (1,2) and (2,1) remain gray despite clicking them. My coordinates may have been slightly off. Need to verify exact positions and click again.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 115 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 100 actions returned the board to a state already seen 100 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 100 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The segmentation shows all 9 cells are now green (color 'N'). But the level is still not complete. Let me check the current frame more carefully - maybe the grid reset or something changed.
Looking at the images provided:
- Step 112: X pattern (5 green)
- Step 113: 6 green (added one)
- Step 114: 7 green (added one)
- Step 115: 8 green (added one)
But the segmentation says all 9 are green now. The images might be from before my latest action. Let me check the current state properly.
Wait, the images shown are the "recent grid images" which are from before my latest tool call. My latest tool call detected 9 green cells and 0 gray cells. But the level is still not complete.
So filling all 9 cells green doesn't complete the level. I need to think about what else the goal could be.
Let me reconsider the puzzle:
- Left panel: 5x5 grid with purple diamond pattern
- Right panel: 3x3 grid that I can click to change colors
Maybe the goal isn't to fill all cells green. Maybe I need to match a specific pattern. The purple pattern on the left is:
. . . . .
. . P . .
. P . P .
. . P . .
. . . . .
This is a diamond/cross pattern. If I map this to a 3x3 grid, it would be:
. P .
P . P
. P .
Which is the cross pattern (5 cells). But earlier when I tried this pattern (clicking the 4 white cells), it caused a reset.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the issue is that I need to click cells in a specific order, or maybe I need to click cells to toggle them (green to gray or vice versa).
Actually, let me check if clicking green cells turns them back to gray. If so, maybe I need to create a specific pattern by toggling cells.
Let me try clicking one of the green cells to see if it toggles back to gray.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: All 9 cells are now green but level still not complete. Filling all green doesn't solve it. Need to test if clicking toggles cells back to gray, suggesting I need a specific pattern rather than all green.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state - all 9 green but not done
# Test if clicking toggles cells back to gray
# Try clicking center cell (1,1) at row 55, col 30

action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 30}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After clicking center (1,1):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    r_center = sum(rows) / len(rows)
    c_center = sum(cols) / len(cols)
    if 47 <= r_center <= 63 and 22 <= c_center <= 38 and n['pixels'] <= 10:
        print(f"  ({r_center:.0f}, {c_center:.0f}): {n['color']}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
